headline: Palm Oil's Brussels Problem Is Really a Governance Problem
context: The EU Deforestation Regulation took effect in December 2025, requiring supply chain traceability that 62% of Malaysian smallholders cannot currently provide.

cards.big: Europe didn't ban palm oil. They banned untraceable palm oil.
cards.sub: The regulation requires proof of origin. 62% of smallholders lack GPS-mapped boundaries.

cards.big: MPOB had seven years' warning. Smallholder mapping: 38% complete.
cards.sub: Large plantations are compliant. The cost falls on the 600,000 smallholders left behind.

cards.big: Malaysia threatened WTO action. The regulation passed anyway.
cards.sub: Diplomatic response focused on sovereignty, not compliance. The market moved without us.

cards.big: Deforestation in Sabah and Sarawak is documented by Malaysia's own satellite data.
cards.sub: FDPM data contradicts MPOB sustainability claims. Two agencies, two truths.

cards.big: The threat is not European regulation. It is our own data gap.
cards.sub: Smallholders who can prove sustainability will access premium markets. Most cannot prove it.

cards.big: Sovereignty arguments do not move commodity buyers. Traceability infrastructure does. Malaysia chose the argument over the infrastructure.
